segments.text: Morning, James.
Morning, now.
Morning, James. Is that good for
you?
Sure.
That's
that.
Yes.
Well, welcome James and your team to the Select Committee this
morning for the Annual Review. Um, look we have got a
structured agenda here, which, um, I'm sure that you're all familiar with.
Um, and we will try and stick to that as much as we can so we can have some
structure to the four hours that you have with us today.
Um, so look, we'll open it up to you to perhaps, uh, make some
introductions and, uh, give us some insights and then, uh, we'll open
up for questions.
Thank you, Chair. Uh, ǁta mārie, kei koutou.
Uh, very good morning, committee.
Um, we've got a good team here from, uh, the Ministry.
Uh, we've got senior lea- leadership team here, uh, and a couple of extra key
staff. Uh, it's a full turn-out this morning,
reflecting, uh, the scale of the work that's been, uh, undertaken this last
year. And I just have to start by saying it's my particular privilege
to be, uh, here this morning. Uh, having the
opportunity to reflect on, uh, the year that was, uh, for,
uh, the Ministry for the Environment for '23/'24.
It was a significant year of, uh, delivery during a
period of change and some challenge.
We started the financial year with a, uh, all-staff
consultation on a major re-organization, uh, to reflect the reduction
in, uh, spending by the agency of, uh, 240 million the year
before down to 180, uh, uh, in the year that that has
been. Um, that consultation resulted
ultimately in 278 redundancies, uh, in the
Ministry, and our, um, permanent, uh, workforce
reduced from 824 down to
686, uh, as of 30- 31 October this year.
So, significant amount of change and many of our staff, uh, had changes
in roles, changes in teams, changes in reporting lines.
And we, uh, we migrated to
a, uh, a, a smaller organization and a structure to reflect
that. Uh, but notwithstanding that, uh, the work continued
and, uh, in October, we passed the, um...
(clears throat)
No, we sort of supported the government and supported, um, uh, Select Committee in
passing the, uh, first Resource Management Reform, uh, Bill.
In December, we introduced the second, uh, Resource Management, uh,
Reform Bill, uh, which was enacted in August of this year.
Um, the Fast-Track Approvals, uh, Act was enacted in December,
uh, and went live in February. And we worked with colleagues across agencies and
the Environmental Protection Authority, in particular, to stand up that.
Uh, while we were doing that, we're working with the expert advisory group on
replacing the Resource Management Act.
And, uh, supported them in producing their report.
Um, we were developing the largest package of national direction changes
under the RMA ever undertaken by, uh, any government.
And consultation, uh, was i-initiated in May on
that. Uh, we also, uh, provided a large amount of
advice to Minister Bishop and Undersecretary Court on,
uh, the, uh, soon to be introduced, uh, Planning and Natural
Environment Bills. On the climate side, uh, we supported the
Government to develop a climate strategy, uh, the second Emiss- Emissions Reduction
Plan, uh, the tabling of a second nationally determined
contribution. Uh, we undertook the first biannual transparency
recor- r- report, uh, required under, um, the Paris
Agreement. We, uh, supported the government to introduce, uh,
Climate Change Response Act, uh, Amendment Bill for forestry conversion.
Uh...
We undertook the review of the 2050, uh, target.
Um, there were the annual ETS settings, uh, consultations
and, uh, and decisions, uh, and also, um, further
review of the Climate Change Response Act.
Uh, the adaptation, uh, work continued and, uh, we
supported the independent advisory group, uh, in producing their report
for, uh, ministers and then ultimately decisions by Cabinet, uh, which
were reflected in the announcements earlier this year and are coming through, uh,
legislative reform, uh, for that adaptation framework.
We continue to support the Maori Climate Platform and got away, um, a
significant number of projects, uh, around the country on that.
Um, as I think has been, uh, announced in, in the last 24 hours
or so, um, we were also working with Ministry for Regulation on reviewing the
Hazardous Substances and New Organisms Act in respect of agricultural
compounds. Uh, and that is now resulting in, um,
uh, the sort of drafting that will come forward.
Uh, we stood up the Tiewise scheme and that's been hugely, uh, successful,
uh, lots of years of work building towards that.
Um, and we have been continuing to progress our refrigerant gases and
agricultural plastics, uh, product stewardship
scheme. Uh, we produced Our Air, uh, uh, and, uh, Our
Environment 2025, which is our three yearly, uh, State of Environment
report, uh, for New Zealand. Also, uh, procured,
uh, a bunch of digital, um, data platforms
including, uh, national, uh, climate, uh,
hazard, uh, information, uh, with, uh,
NIWA. Um, overall, uh, I have to say it was a
record, uh, volume of, uh, deliverables by the
Ministry. Um, uh, despite the, uh, reduction
in resources, uh, overall-
(clears throat) .
Uh, the Ministry produced, uh, in terms of outputs to ministers,
parliament, uh, and the public, uh,
4,822, um, uh, items which
included 896 briefings, 62 Cabinet papers,
452 O- OAs, um, and many more,
um, parliamentary questions and ministerial pieces or ministerial co-
correspondence.Uh, while doing all of that, um,
as is reflected in the annual report, uh, our quality of advice has
improved and we have, um, continued to progress, uh, on our, um,
target metrics there. Um, our turnaround, uh, on
OAAs, we've met targets there. Ministerial satisfaction is up.
Um, we have enhanced overall the evidence base for the ministry, um,
and we have int- introduced a much more centralized, um, organizational,
um, business planning and performance monitoring, uh, system, which is really
showing, uh, results. Uh, and we did really set out the year
as a senior leadership team with the organization as saying after organizational
change it was going to be a year of performance, uh, and we were really gonna focus
on, uh, getting as- as much, uh, delivery from the organization with
our increasingly scarce resources as possible.
Um, so I'm just delighted with, uh, the annual report that, uh, has
been, uh, produced, because it, uh, I think provides a good summary
of that. And also as you will have heard from our auditors, uh, we've continued to
progress, um, the quality of our financial systems and controls from good
now to very good. Uh, and just note that, um, in terms of
the government's target of 10-day, uh, processing of
invoices, we're at 95.9%, so over target
there, uh, and leading the government in electronic
invoicing. Uh, management, um, control environment,
uh, uh, has also maintained as very good.
Uh, and then in performance information we're still at good, and I hope by next
year we'll be very good on that too, and it has been noted by the auditors, um, as
well as our independent audit risk committee, um, that the ministry's come a long
way in terms of its non-financial reporting, and hopefully that's useful, uh, to
the committee. Um, there's been a huge amount of work to- to ensure that's as
meaningful as possible. So, um, uh, Madam Chair, in
summary, uh, what I would just, uh, draw the committee's attention to
is the fact that the ministry, uh, has worked with less, has
delivered more, uh, and, uh, has done so with high
quality, uh, of, uh, outputs and performance.
And I think, uh, that is a enormous credit to, um, the good
people of the Ministry for the Environment.
They have worked extraordinarily hard, incredibly, uh, professionally, and
with dedication, uh, to public service.
Mm-hmm.
So I'm enormously proud of them and very grateful, uh, for their work this year.
Thank you.
Well, thank you, James, and thank you to your team.
It's always good to hear that, you know, you're working with less but producing
more. So, uh, yeah, that's really efficient and good.
But look, we've got lots of questions, so, uh, we'll start with
Rachel.
Uh, thank you, uh, and congratulations to your team, who I know are
working very hard, and we obviously see them a lot here at this
committee. But, um, I'm wondering in terms of this
statement that they're doing, um, you're working with less but
you're doing more, seems that there's a lot more stress as well.
So the... Uh... Um,
we've got here the figure of 54% of
staff, um, often, um, reporting stress.
Do you- do you think that's to do with the less and more
relationship?
I think it is to do with the, uh, the pace at which we're working, um,
and, uh, that has been the case for a number of years
now. Uh, and I do think, and I have said this to the committee
before, uh, I do think that the cadence of the New Zealand parliamentary
system, uh, particularly the three-year term-
Mm-hmm.
... uh, does result in- uh, in governments on all sides of the house, uh,
moving at pace. And I do recognize that we're often addressing, uh,
urgent issues for New Zealanders and that everybody is ambitious
for, uh, delivery, um, but it is certainly true, uh,
that the- that the pace at which the public service is- is now working,
uh, and I think particularly, um, since the COVID era, uh, we've got
very agile and very good at running at sprints, uh, you know, it does
put, uh, significant pressure on our people.
So th- that is a- um, an area of close focus and concern for the senior leadership
team, and we have a number of initiatives in place to, um, to support our staff
in that regard, um, but there are things that we can't control and those are
particularly the time frames that the parliamentary process, uh, demands of
us, including, you know, a big legislative volume.
Just a supplement.
Y- yes, Lum.
Thank you. Um, and thank you for your work, James, and acknowledging
your resignation as well and your mo- movement.
That's really exciting, to live in your role.
Um, I just wanted to pick up on, you mention in your report, you describe it
as an unprecedented pace of
change. What outcome... Like, what do you think this actually
means? You've mentioned the staff impacts.
What about the actual outcomes that you're delivering in terms of your work
with this unprecedented pace?
Well, I think, um, that, look, the biggest body of work that we've undertaken has
been the replacement of the Resource Management Act, uh, and, uh,
I think, um, there's an awful lot of work for select committee,
and I assume it will, uh, be this select committee, uh, to,
uh, further work through that legislation.
So, I think there's always a, um, uh, there's a trade-off for a
government in- in how much deliberation you might do, how much consultation you
might do in the development of policy and in legislation before it reaches the
house, and how much do you rely on the select committee process.
Um, uh, we have, uh, at the, um, request
of government, uh, worked very quickly to bring legislation before the
house, and so, um, in- in that regard, uh, the legislation
will, uh, require further refinement, uh, in the select committee process, and I
think this will be a case of where the select committee is going to have to do, uh,
more of the heavy lifting than it might have done if we had perhaps a-
another whole parliamentary term to quietly work up some
proposals, consult on them more broadly, um, stress test them, et
cetera. And I think that's the- the balance of- of workload really
between-... the executive and the, and the legislature.
Mm-hmm. Thank you. Look, we will take a few more sups on this.
I'm just, um, for those following, um, we are looking at overall
performance. Um, that's the topic that we're currently on, so
we'll just keep the question lines to that kind of area before we move on to RMA
and other areas. Um, Chloe?
Yeah, so just particularly on that point around the balance in select committee and
legislature executive, uh, one of the big pieces of legislative reform
that's coming down the pipeline is, of course, the halving of the methane targets.
Will that bill have a select committee process?
Uh, uh, the, uh, Climate Change Response
Act will go through a select committee process.
Um, there will be, uh, amendments to, uh, the
legislation that may be progressed on a, uh, on a tighter timeframe.
So the Climate Change Response Act, so, so just to understand how many pieces of
leg might flow from all of the announcements-
Mm-hmm.
... that have come out over the last month or so, particularly here, I'm
talking about the halving of the biogenic-
Yeah.
... methane target. So that piece of legislation is
encapsulated with the other reforms that are occurring as well, or has it got its
own specific piece of leg?
It's being introduced together as-
Yeah.
... as one package. Sam, what's the status of-
So there are some-
... the ministers?
There are some options that Ministers have.
Uh, so there's a combination of, um, uh,
policy decisions, including the methane target-
Mm-hmm.
... along with the package changes that were announced recently
related to the CCRA-
Yep.
... as well as adaptation planning, uh, legislative requirements.
And so there are some options that ministers need to-
Still make decisions on.
... work through to make sure the final mix, um, legislative
mix is sorted as to whether or not, um, it goes in
one or two packages. But I would expect that there's likely to be two pieces of
legislation. And the intention has been to, um,
uh, move on and legislate pretty quickly on the, on the
methane target.
I mean, noting that we've only got two more sitting weeks of the-
Yeah. (laughs)
... House left this year, so am I hearing from MFE that there will be a
select committee process for the biogenic methane target?
That's not, that's not our decision to make.
Okay. But have you provided advice that hasn't, has said that there is possibility
for there to be a select committee process for it?
Uh, depend, depends on the time frames and the approach.
It's up to ministers as to what process they want.
Okay, so what I'm hearing is that you've said that it is possible, but it is up to
ministers to use their discretion.
Correct.
Okay. So that basically means there could potentially be public input
into that biogenic methane target being
reset.
I think what we can say is that, that there will be a legislative vehicle to amend
the Climate Change Response Act going to select committee-
Mm-hmm.
... next year, and that is a vehicle, um, that could have encapsulated those
issues. But those are choices for ministers.
Great. Thank you. And Grant has a supplementary.
Yeah, thank you, um, James, and ack- acknowledge the, obviously
the work, uh, that staff, um, contribute over the last
couple of years in particular. I mean, as a businessperson myself, farmer,
I appreciate doing, uh, more with less, comes with, comes with the
territory, so I really appreciate it. Thank you.
Um, now just, you talk about the RMA and the amount of time,
um, uh, the, the, the focus on that and the amount of
effort that's gone into that, and the fact you, you, you, you...
The point I want to make is, given the importance of the RMA for every aspect
of, of, of New Zealanders' lives, whether it's creating renewable energy to help
deal with power pri-, you know, the power supply issues in this country-
Mm-hmm.
... housing costs and so forth, do you think it's, you know...
And do you not think that's appropriate then to say, "Okay, yes, we
acknowledge it's been a lot of work, but the importance to the country is such that
we actually then need to get on and achieve these things for, for the wider good of
the community"?
Certainly in our advice to ministers, uh, we have,
uh, we have recommended,
uh, that the system trans- transition to the new system
as quickly as possible.
Mm-hmm.
Uh, and that was something that was discussed with the expert advisory group as
being desirable overall. The longer it takes,
uh, the more, uh, confusion there will be of running dual
systems, uh, and inertia potentially created in
the implementation. So certainly the minister- the ministry has
recommended, uh, taking a, a rapid approach to the
implementation. The policy development, notwithstanding my earlier
comments about how rapidly the work has been done in the last year-
Mm-hmm.
... did benefit from the previous government's reforms and
reviews. So the ministry has had now five-plus
years of solid RMA, uh, reconsideration,
including the input of a range of advisory groups, et cetera.
So, um, there are elements of the reforms that will be
introduced, uh, in the next week by the, uh, government that will be
common to, uh, the previous reforms and will buil- build on those
reforms. Uh, and there will be, um, areas of distinction as well.
So I think, uh, I think we've got a really solid foundation, uh, both as a
ministry but also as a country in terms of understanding what are the
issues with the performance of the Resource Management Act and what are some of
those solutions. And so, um, I'm really confident with the
legislative package that's coming to the committee next week, uh, as being
something that, uh, is workable-
Yep.
... um, but it will benefit from, uh, the, the, the select committee's
process, certainly.
Thank you. Appreciate it.
And Lan?
Thank you. Um, we've had the Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment
very helpfully,
uh, inform us about the change in overall budgeted
expenditure for the environment.
And he's pointed out that in the previous financial year, this annual
review year, it was at 36, sorry, 3.6
billion and is projected for this financial year to be
moving to 2.6 billion. And Forest and Bird have very
helpfully pointed out that that's essentially equal to one
urban roading-
... development project. Do you feel that the
environment is being deprioritized within these, within
government's decisions?
I think those funding changes reflect, uh,
some, uh, time-limited funding, certainly what came through the Jobs For Nature
program, uh, but also, uh, the one-off funding
associated with, uh, recovery from severe weather, Cyclone
Gabrielle. And I know that embedded within those numbers were,
um, uh, the funding for buyouts, for example.
So, um, I think there are some contextual,
uh, drivers for that reduction. Um, I think, look,
overall, uh, we have seen an increase in
institutional capability, and I think the Parliamentary Commissioner's advice is
that, um, the work that, uh,
the, um, the ministries and departments, uh, of government that work in this area
have continued to build capability.
It's been the on-the-ground delivery where, um, the
time-limited funding has particularly come to an end.
And so, I think in those areas, particularly in constrained economic
times, uh, not only for the government's books but also for businesses and
business owners and for communities, um, it's not
surprising that there is less to go around.
And you'll hear that in the voluntary sector as well as hearing that, uh, in the
private sector. So, I think that is a point in the economic cycle.
Uh, and as we come out of that, there are opportunities for everybody to
invest more, uh, in the environment.
And a supplementary.
Thank you. So, just on that, what do you think it says
about New Zealand's, I guess, lack of investment in
environmental expenditure when a few
climate-charged weather events can cause such
a disruption and such a change in our
expenditure when we know that we're gonna have this,
these kinds of events happen more frequently in the future?
The ministry, uh, uh, regularly
surveys, uh, public opinion on environmental matters, uh, and
that's, uh, important information for us to inform,
uh, ministers, uh, about, uh, public sentiment, and also
for us to understand the, um, the public expectation of our
work. And what we have seen, uh, over the last couple of
years as a consequence of the stress on households and, um,
businesses and communities has been a reduction in the priority
of, uh, environmental action and investment in the environment for New
Zealanders, um, and I think that just reflects, uh, uh,
the, the, the priorities and the, and the issues that, that, that people are
facing. So again, I think it's, uh, time and place, um,
and in my role, um, I'm incredibly,
um, uh, uh, sympathetic to the fact that
for most New Zealanders, uh, there are more near-term, uh,
pressing priorities for them. Uh, we obviously, uh,
see a lot of data, uh, that talks to long-term,
uh, systemic challenges for the country, um, and those exist in other
areas outside of the environment as well.
Mm-hmm.
And I think the challenge for, uh, for New Zealand, um, particularly
given our, uh, relatively,
um, poor economic performance in the last few years, is to
sustain investment in a whole range of areas that require a long-term, uh,
focus. And I just think that's hard for New Zealanders.
It's also, also it's hard for a three-year electoral cycle.
Mm-hmm. Thank you, and Rachel.
Well, a supplementary on that. Relative to
whom? You just said that we'd had a poor
economic, relatively poor economic
times.
So-
Isn't the whole world?
Yeah, so, so
rel- relative to p- previous periods.
Yeah. Okay, thank you. Uh, a different question,
um, we've talked about this reduction in staff, and
has ... Can you tell us if it's created any delays,
um, or risks in f- fulfilling statutory
obligations?
We ... Uh, so, so I think the s- the short answer to that
is no. Uh, we have focused, uh, and we focused
very heavily on, uh, developing our new operating model
and our new organizational structure on ensuring we could meet our statutory
requirements. The contraction was more, uh, in the, the
work that had grown over time that you could say is more discretionary,
uh, particularly related to those time-limited programs, uh,
and the level of community engagement and outreach that, that we
might do. Um, so it is our core-
Oh,
OK. ... uh, statutory functions that we continue to prioritize.
Thank you. And so, if, uh, the, the
people are going more at the policy end, a- acknowledging that you've just said
community engagement as well, uh, are you able to give free and
frank advice to ministers?
Yes.
And have you observed any instances of ministerial
requests to alter or reshape, um, your advice to
align with their political priorities?
No.
Okay, thank you. Um,
and
the, uh, further reductions in staff,
what, where, what parts of the ministry
will they, will be affected by those, those reductions?
So, at the moment, we've, we've essentially got a, a, a ...
Well, we do have a freeze on, uh, recruiting new permanent
staff except by, um, uh, absolute exception, and that
reflects the fact that there is a declining baseline in, in out
years.
Yeah.
But we also, uh, have, um,
a, a program business case which has been, uh, publicly acknowledged by Minister
Bishop-... to fund the, uh, implementation of the government's
resource management reforms. That will require a surge in
resources in the ministry on a time-limited basis, so we do,
uh, anticipate that that reduction in overall headcount will,
will plateau, uh, and that we will maintain a, um,
similar wor- size workforce to what we have today over the next couple of years to
deliver that work. That is all still subject to Budget 26 decision-making,
so I can't be certain of that-
Of
course. ... um, but we're essentially holding, holding, uh, still at the moment until we
get certainty around that.
Okay, so you are expecting there will be
more resource for the implementation of RMA reforms,
but that more resource will just be that you don't reduce as much as you
were planning to reduce?
There are some, there are some, uh, uh, additional skills, uh, and
capabilities that we will need to bring into the ministry.
Mm-hmm.
Uh, the reforms are predicated on a digitally-enabled
system.
Mm-hmm.
Uh, a data-heavy system, uh, data-informed,
so, uh, we, we will be needing to bring in, uh, new skills into the
ministry. Um, we do have, uh, digital capabilities, but certainly
not of the, um, of the scale required to fully
digitalize New Zealand's resource management system.
Okay. That sounds expensive.
It can be.
Because (whispers)
It is. Yes, although I... Look, I would just say, uh, to the member that the,
the work we've done on the economic, economic benefits of
the, um, digitization and the overall transformation of the system are
significant.
Yeah.
So we do believe that there are significant productivity benefits for New Zealand
from moving to a digital-
Yeah.
... a digital-first-
Excellent.
... planning system.
Thank
you. And I don't disagree with that. Oh, gosh.
Thank you. We've got a couple more supplementaries. Uh, Ryan.
Uh, thanks, Chair. Look, it just opened up something that's quite exciting.
When you talk about the digital potential that's coming, are you able to speak more
about it without getting ahead of next week's reforms?
Uh, I'd perhaps just decline that one-
Mm-hmm.
... given that there will be announcements by Minister Bishop next week and I don't
want to steal his thunder.
Yeah, thank you very much.
Okay.
It's just a little teaser I picked up.
And, uh, Priyanka.
Thank you. Mine could really be a sup, and it's on, uh, the staffing
numbers that you have-
Mm-hmm. ... in your responses to written questions.
And I see that your fixed-term and your headcount have gone down, as you've
mentioned, but, and I also take the point that contracting has changed in
terms of how you re-, um, your recording of
contractors, but it has more than doubled, um, of,
since the 20, you know, between the 23/24
years and the 24/25. So can you explain what, what
that's about?
The contractor expenditure?
Yeah.
Uh, the 23/24 year, uh, was,
uh, the year in which we completed the resource management reforms and we'd had a
very large, uh, program of, uh, contracting, uh, up until that
point, and it was obviously during the time of change of government,
so, um, uh, the overall reform,
uh, work of the ministry, uh, was largely on hold during that
period, and so we did have a significant drop in our contracting, uh,
requirements. Um, as we have come back into, uh,
the, um, the, the reform program, uh, the renewed reform
program, that has required us to contract in, uh, some additional
resource. Um, uh, but overall, we're still
below, uh, well below where we were, uh, uh, historically,
um, uh, during the previous, uh, period of reform, and we're certainly within
the, uh, the cap that's been set for us by the Public Service Commission.
So would you say that it's, um, reasonably, uh,
common practice or usual practice that
agencies, ministries would contract out,
um, or increase spending on contractors when there are
time-limited projects, rather than adding to your
fixed-term headcount?
So, so certainly, uh, when there is a surge because there is
a major reform piece of work to do, for example,
um, uh, certainly doesn't make sense to try to procure
permanent employees-
Mm-hmm.
... when that is, uh, the work is fixed-term and the, and the funding is
fixed-term. So yes, we complement, uh,
We also use fixed-term employees as well, um, and a mix of both
contractors and fixed-term employees to manage that workload,
uh, uh, peak.
Just coming back to that staffing though, 'cause I think it's quite an interesting
that, you know, obviously the ministry had kind of gone from 300 to 1,000
quite quickly and then obviously come back, um, a, a
few hundred and, but then obviously you're still managing your workload
and, um, you know, producing, uh, uh, more efficiently
and streamlined. So, um, has the workload,
um, increased
to... And then do you feel that the numbers are round where they should be
currently?
So, look, the, the workload has increased, uh, well, it went, it went
through a di- through change of government and has, has got back
to comparable levels, uh, as, as it was with the
previous government in policy and legislative reform.
So we are, we are really at, at, at full noise on that.
Yeah.
Uh, it is in the area of, uh, community engagement as, as I
suggested before, and administration of, uh, particular funds,
uh, spent on the ground with community groups, and obviously the Jobs For Nature,
uh, is a, is an example of that, where there has been the ending of
those and there has been, um, some retrenchment of the ministry's
staffing and capability, uh, to support that sort of work.
So, um, it's not exactly an apples for apples, uh,
comparison between, um, the, the parliamentary terms, if you
like. Um, what we've just had to do is, uh, work within our means and
also, uh, deliver the work. That has resulted in the organization, uh,
working smarter. Um, we are making use of, uh,
technology. Uh, we are, have also changed, uh, some of the ways
in which, um, the organization deploys r- resource internally so it is more
flexible, uh, agile, um, more cross-organizational teams
to, to deliver.
Mm-hmm.
And I think, look, over time I think every organization gets, gets kind of
better and better at sweating its resources, um, and there
has been, um, a, a, a considerable amount of innovation within the way we
work, uh, and that has helped deliver, uh, more with less as well.
Yeah. Brilliant, thank you. Um, Laan?
Can I move on to strategic priorities?
Um-
Just checking there.
